name: Sinus Tachycardia
identifyBy: Upright P waves in II precede every QRS
P waves may merge with preceding T wave at high rates
Rate is appropriate to physiological state (pain, fever, anxiety, exercise)
Gradual onset and offset - distinguishes from paroxysmal SVT

measurements.pWaves: Upright II - may be hidden in T wave at fast rates

management: Treat the underlying cause - sinus tachycardia is a response, not a primary rhythm
Causes: pain, fever, anxiety, hypovolaemia, anaemia, hyperthyroidism, PE, sepsis, heart failure
Do NOT use AV nodal blocking drugs (adenosine, verapamil) - will not terminate and can cause hypotension
Beta-blockers only if thyrotoxicosis or specific indication - not general first-line
pearls: Sinus tachycardia is never the primary problem - always find the cause
Rate >150 bpm: P waves may be hidden in T waves - compare multiple leads to find them
Inappropriate sinus tachycardia (IST): persistent elevated rate without physiological trigger - rare, diagnosis of exclusion
After exercise, heart rate should decrease within 1 minute - failure to do so suggests chronotropic dysfunction
PE classically causes sinus tachycardia - not necessarily any other arrhythmia

qaNotes: Sinus rhythm family: P before every QRS unless sinus-node dysfunction pause pattern is specified.
